title: Draught-sealing a letterbox
subtitle: Brush insert and inner cover plate, fifteen minutes
excerpt: A standard UK letterbox slot loses more heat per square metre than almost any other gap in the building envelope. A brush insert and an inner cover plate reduce that loss to near zero. The job takes fifteen minutes and costs .
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance. Standard UK letterbox dimensions: 254 mm wide × 38 mm tall (BS EN 13724).

content.text: A letterbox slot is an uninsulated hole through the front door. The standard BS EN 13724 aperture is 254 × 38 mm, small, but exposed to wind-driven air. On a house facing a prevailing westerly, even a well-fitting outer flap lets cold air through on a windy day; a worn or absent flap makes it considerably worse.

Two parts give the best result. First, a brush insert with nylon bristles that span the slot from inside the door. Second, an inner cover plate, an insulated flap hinged behind the outer mechanism. Either part on its own is a real improvement. Both together close the gap to almost nothing.
Letterbox brush insert, a nylon-bristle frame that clamps behind the outer plate. Fits most standard UK letterboxes (254 mm wide) without adaptation.
Inner cover plate (optional but recommended), an insulated flap mounted on the inner face of the door, hinged to open for mail delivery. Adds a second air barrier.
Step 1, remove the inner letterbox plate.
From inside the door, unscrew the inner letterbox plate (usually two or four screws). Set it aside.
Step 2, feed the brush insert through the slot.

Angle the brush insert through the letterbox slot from inside, so the brushes are at the front (visible when you look through from outside) and the fixing frame is on the inside face. The brushes should span the full width and height of the slot.

Align the brush insert screw holes with the existing inner plate holes (or use the supplied screws and backing plate if the kit provides one). Tighten firmly, overtightening can crack a plastic surround, so snug is enough.
Replace the inner letterbox plate over the brush insert. Confirm the outer flap still opens and closes freely from outside.

If adding an inner cover plate, mount it on the inside door face, centred over the letterbox opening. Mark the fixing holes, pre-drill in the door timber (not through the door, check door thickness first), and fix with the supplied screws. The hinged flap should hang closed at rest and swing open when mail is pushed through.

Hold the back of your hand in front of the letterbox on a cold day. Any remaining draught indicates a gap around the insert frame or an ill-fitting cover plate. Check the outer flap is not held open by a worn spring, a broken outer flap loses as much heat as no flap at all.

items.fix: Check the outer flap opens freely before fitting the insert; lightly oil a stiff pivot if needed.
items.cause: Brush insert too thick or outer flap mechanism stiff
items.symptom: Outer flap won't open for mail delivery

items.fix: Confirm insert is the correct size for your letterbox; add a bead of flexible sealant around the insert frame edge on the inside face.
items.cause: Brush bristles not spanning the full slot width, or gap around the insert frame
items.symptom: Draught still apparent with insert fitted
